speaker: interviewer
text: --on March 24 of 1996 in Chevy Chase, Maryland, with the interview of Drexel Sprecher by Bruce Einhorn, in English. Why don't you tell me what the charges were in those cases, which you have called the industrialist and economics cases? Why don't you go through them, tell me what the charges were in all those cases in which you were a supervisor?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the case of the-- IG Farben, the-- we had a charge of aggressive war, which we did not have in the Flick case. The Flick case came first-- the first industrialist case. And in that charge-- in that case, the leading members of the Flick Concern were charged with using vast numbers of slave laborers, gathered from the west and from the eastern countries, and brought into Germany, and in some cases, treated fairly decently, and other cases, treated very badly. So that was the one count against the Flick Concern. The second count of the Flick Concern was the taking over of some foreign properties beyond the requirements needed for the-- the maintenance of the occupied Army of Germany. And that was the rule that-- that an-- under traditional law, you can demand of the occupied country what's required to help you sustain your army that's occupying. But you can't go beyond that in terms of supplying your people back home with riches, or with spoliated goods. The case against IG Farben had not only spoliation and slave labor charges, but the charge of aggressive war. And this was based largely on the fact that the defendant-- the first defendant in that case, Carl Krauch, K-R-A-U-C-H, [CLEARS THROAT] had been appointed by Goering [CLEARS THROAT] to be the head of the industrialist agencies, almost as if-- it was kind of a super-minister to get war production going. Now, he was not a minister, but he had tremendous powers. And of course, he used his own concern. And while he was in that position of the government, he remained as the-- for some time as the head of the Aufsichtsrat, the supervisory board of the IG Farben Concern. So you had this, what we would call, a real conflict of interest, actually here. But the way it was used in-- in Germany, it just made the industry the servant of the-- of the Nazi Reich. We also charged the-- the defendants in the Krupp case with aggressive war. But in both of these cases, the tribunals did not find the-- any defendant guilty of aggressive war. In each of the cases, there were some findings [CLEARS THROAT] of guilt for slave labor and for spoliation.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were their convictions in all of the cases in which you participated in the subsequent proceedings? There were. I think a third of the defendants in the IG Farben case, or maybe nine altogether, were found guilty on one count or another, on spoiliation or on slave labor, the use of concentration camp labor. But over half the defendants in the Farben case were found not guilty on any count. In the Flick case, I don't remember the exact figures, but I would think, of about seven or eight defendants, all, except perhaps one, were found guilty of slave labor or spoliation. What were the range of sentences your convicted defendants received in the subsequent proceedings?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were, in the-- in the medical case and in the Einsatzgruppen case, I believe, some of the defendants were sentenced to-- to death. And in the medical case, those sentenced to death, that was-- they-- those sentences were carried out. But in the later cases, there was appeals to the clemency-- to the military governor, McCloy. And he appointed a commission, which didn't consult the representatives of the prosecution or any of the former prosecutors, but what they thought. And a lot of them said there's variation in these sentences. They ought to be equalized downward, toward the-- the-- the lesser sentences. And so, there were commutations, eventually, so that 10 years after the-- 10 years after the subsequent proceedings, hardly any of the remaining defendants were in jail. A few of them, as I say, in the medical case and in, I believe, the Einsatzgruppen case, were sentenced to death. I had no direct responsibility for either of those cases, so I-- I'm-- my memory of the detail is not that great.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you just tell us what Einsatzgruppen means?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there's hardly a translation for that. [CLEARS THROAT] But it's-- the best we could do is call it 'Special Action Group'. Einsatz really means substitute, mainly, in German. And what it really meant was a special group designated to carry out some functions. And the main functions of the Einsatz groups, which were established under Himmler and the SS, were to fight the opposition, to go into the occupied countries and find the opponents-- and, of course, Jews and gypsies-- to run the concentration camps.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of judges did you have in the subsequent proceedings? Well, as-- as I-- [CLEARS THROAT] as I've said, there-- only in one-- in only two, and at the most three cases, were there death sentences. There were a number of sentences for life. Most of them were not served, they were commuted by McCloy later on. The judges themselves, though-- what kind of judges did you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I thought you said-- well, the judges in the subsequent proceedings were mainly state court judges.

speaker: interviewer
text: From what country?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, all of them were from the United States. The subsequent proceedings were entirely conducted-- well, with one exception, I can tell you-- were conducted entirely by Americans. And that meant American prosecutors and American judges. Now, one of the exceptions is in the case of-- the ministry's case. A friend of mine in the French delegation, Charles Gertoffer, G-E-R-T-H-O-F-F-E-R, who had worked in the first case, he and I became quite friendly. And he was brought in to present some part of one of the cases, partly because Telford Taylor just wanted to be able to say we used someone beside Americans. In that case, we used Charles Gerthoffer.

speaker: interviewer
text: And by ministry's case, you mean--

speaker: interviewee
text: A case that-- it was the 11th of the 12 cases. It was the last in which there was a judgment. It was the longest case in Nuremberg. It was longer than the first trial of the-- before the International Military Tribunal. And it had in it 24, or 23, or 22 defendants, most of whom were high officials in the Nazi government.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was your boss during the subsequent proceedings?

speaker: interviewee
text: Colonel and then General Telford Taylor.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was his title?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was the successor to Justice Jackson, and hence was called, US Chief of Council for War Crimes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your impression of General Taylor?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a remarkable man. Justice Jackson had picked him out long before the war as a very able writer of briefs and a very able assistant. And he'd gotten him into the Department of Justice, when he was Attorney General, and so on. And during the war, Telford Taylor had gotten into intelligence at a very high rank. And he'd become a full colonel. And he was in Bletchley, England, where the intelligence staff that intercepted the German broadcasts and the German code-- coded messages, they broke the code and so on. So he was working with intelligence over there. And when Jackson was appointed Chief of Council for War Crimes by President Truman, within a week, [CLEARS THROAT] Justice Jackson got the War Department to order Telford Taylor to come back and become one of his principal deputies. And Taylor didn't even know why, but he was being shipped back to the States. And-- and he became very-- immediately, one of the very close deputies to Justice Jackson. And I think that the trial-- the main-- the first trial had not been underway more than a month, before Jackson had it in his mind Taylor is the man I'm going to recommend to succeed me, to handle the next trials after the first trial.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you maintained contact with General Taylor since the end of the Nuremberg Trials?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Since I was the editor in chief of the official volumes on those trials, [COUGHS] I submitted them to-- I submitted drafts of some parts of those volumes to him, because he's a very great linguist, and he could improve, frankly, my diction. And after I had finished [CLEARS THROAT] editing these volumes, or supervising the editing of them, General Taylor was made head of The Small Defense Plants Administration in about 1951.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the US?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the United States. And [CLEARS THROAT] he then called on me to become assistant administrator of the Small Defense Plants Administration. So by that time, I was done editing the trials and getting the publications, which you can see up here on the shelf-- 15 volumes, in those green volumes. And so I moved over and became assistant administrator, under Telford Taylor, of the Small Defense Plants Administration.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you prepared the publication of the trial proceeding volumes, were you still in Nuremberg?

speaker: interviewee
text: Both. [CLEARS THROAT] I was both in Nuremberg and in Washington. At one time, in-- [CLEARS THROAT] in Germany, in Nuremberg, I must have had over 50 people working for me, making recommendations and assembling evidence to be put into volumes where the total record would not be published, because that was in mimeograph form, and it would be hundreds of thousands of pages. So we had to condense this and to put the main things in-- the indictment, and the opening statements, and the closing statements of both the prosecution and the defense, or at least a majority of the-- of the-- those statements, and then extracts from the evidence of both the prosecution, followed by the evidence of the defense. And that's what's published in those 15 green volumes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And during what years did you prepare these volumes?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I-- we-- we started in-- it over in Germany. And we had-- I should say we had over 50 people working on that. And over half of them were Germans. And one of the defense counsel, Karl Hoffman, who had represented the defendant, Ambros, for example, in the IG Farben trial, very gladly came over and became a part of the publication staff. And we relied a great deal on him to be able to say, this is the best defense evidence, which was offered to respond to the prosecution evidence. And so we put a selection in of the prosecution evidence, and we put a selection in of the defense evidence. And these people made their recommendations to me and a couple of my assistants. And then, that's what became the official volumes on the last 12 trials.

speaker: interviewer
text: When were they completed?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think I completed those-- the-- the editing of those volumes in late 1951, or maybe early in '51. I can't remember, just now.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did you leave Nuremberg?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'd left Nuremberg in 1949. So by that time, most of the work had been done in a-- a preliminary sense. And what had to be done back in Washington-- where we worked in the Pentagon-- we had a small office in the Pentagon-- the main problem was taking what these 50 people had, and I had, worked on in Nuremberg, and-- and brought over to Washington, and condensing it still more, because we knew we couldn't have more than 15 volumes. It wouldn't be practical in the sense of their being read by a lot of people. And the judge-advocate, or the Army would not have approved printing. It was a very costly business, to-- to print 1,000 volumes or more of volumes that large. Those volumes are all 1,000 pages or more.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you leave the Army?

speaker: interviewee
text: I left the Army, in the sense of being an officer, while I was in Nuremberg. And the reason simply was that, by that time, General Taylor was hiring [CLEARS THROAT] some of his principal deputies at supergrade levels. And that-- that amount of income was a great deal more than what I was getting as a captain. So I, having had overseas service, I had no problem getting released from the Army and accepting a job as a GS-15, and later a GS-16, which is a supergrade. And paid me very well.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was why you were still at Nuremberg?

speaker: interviewee
text: I beg your pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you-- when did you become a civilian?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Nuremberg.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Nuremberg. Did you retire at the rank of Captain?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Now, after you worked with General Taylor [CLEARS THROAT] as his assistant counsel, as a civilian after Nuremberg, with the defense plants industries, what was-- what was your next area of employment?

speaker: interviewee
text: After I'd finished doing the editing of these things?

speaker: interviewer
text: And then, after you worked with him in the defense organization as his assistant council.

speaker: interviewee
text: Say that again, I didn't-- I'm not hearing you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Didn't you say that General Taylor had asked for you to be his deputy in the job he took after Nuremberg?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. [CLEARS THROAT] When the Eisenhower administration came in, why, we went out. We were succeeded by a new administration.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] well, I should-- should go back one, because I was-- there was something I omitted. After I finished editing the Nuremberg Trials, I became associate counsel, the number two counsel, in the Salary Stabilization Board, which is a part of the total stabilization pattern at that time. And Telford Taylor knew I was on that job. And when he was asked to be the administrator by President Truman of the Small Defense Plants Administration, he, very soon thereafter, asked me to become assistant administrator. And that was in 1951, or the beginning of '52.

speaker: interviewer
text: And after you worked with the Small Defense Plants Administration, what was your next position?

speaker: interviewee
text: I [CLEARS THROAT] largely was engaged in real estate ventures and in the-- the Potomac Construction Company out in Potomac, Maryland and around Potomac, Maryland. And Mrs. Sprecher and I bought some land. And we developed the Ridgeland Farm Estates. And we made quite a bit of money doing that.

speaker: interviewee
text: You-- Democratic National Committee, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that was later. I became deputy chairman [CLEARS THROAT] of the Democratic National Committee.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that, sir?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1957. And I continued in that position, until-- until the Kennedy team came in after the convention, when Kennedy was made the nominee. And then, I was replaced as deputy chief counsel-- or deputy administrator of the Democratic National Committee. And I was-- my job then was-- a fellow by the name of Ralph Dungan, who later became president of the main university in New Jersey, what is it? Rutgers?

speaker: interviewer
text: And after you left as vice chair, or deputy chair of the Democratic National Committee, what did you do during the Kennedy years?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had become interested in behavioral science. And I had begun to study a good deal in holding conferences with behavioral scientists, who went out with me [CLEARS THROAT] to help train a bunch of leading Democrats, who would be able to do a better job than they were doing before in relating to people, in developing leaders in the Democratic Party. And this received a fair amount of attention at the Democratic National Convention in 1959, or just before Kennedy was elected. I had to make a speech and have all my six deputies across the country make little talks about what we'd been doing, and so on. After that was over, I-- well, in the process of doing that, I got to know a tremendous number of professors who were in the field of behavioral science. And one of them, Gordon Lippitt, Dr. Gordon Lippitt, suggested that we form a group and use these people in order to run training programs and to charge industry people and other voluntary organizations for training. And that we would conduct these five-day or six-day workshops in which we exposed them to a lot of things that they had missed in their adult education. And these turned out to be very successful. In this firm, we had leadership resources-- continued on for some time. And eventually, I moved from being executive director to being president of the leadership resources. In the meantime, as I say, we had-- I'd inherited a little money. And we invested it in land out in the country, in Montgomery County. And that took some time, as well.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what state?

speaker: interviewee
text: I beg your pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: You said Montgomery County.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What state?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maryland. [CLEARS THROAT]

speaker: interviewer
text: And you mentioned your wife, Virginia. Where did you meet your wife Virginia? Well, when?

speaker: interviewee
text: [CHUCKLES] I met my wife, Virginia, about six months before I left Nuremberg. She was on a tour, [CLEARS THROAT] with a group of Wellesley students and friends of hers, of Europe. And Dr. Russell, the Secretary General of the tribunals, had asked her to come to visit him, just to see what was going on. And she came to Nuremberg. And I was introduced to her. And this started a little correspondence by mail. And when I came back to the States, I pursued her further.

speaker: interviewer
text: When were you married?

speaker: interviewee
text: And eventually, we got married.

speaker: interviewer
text: When? [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] In September-- [HOLDS HAND TO EAR AS IF LISTENING]

words.text: [HOLDS HAND TO EAR AS IF LISTENING]

speaker: interviewee
text: 1949. [CHUCKLES]

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many children have you had?

speaker: interviewee
text: Three.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you think is the lasting lesson that you would like to see learned from your contributions at Nuremberg?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I-- early, as I've explained to you, [CLEARS THROAT] saw, or felt that dictatorship inevitably led to dehumanization of people, that it definitely stopped the progress of the human race, and that it led to wars, and that it led to enslavement. And this irritated the hell out of me. And so I decided to become involved and do what I could, in my own way, the best I could work into things, to counteract that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, Mr. Sprecher, thank you very much. And a very happy birthday, just a couple of days in advance, to you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you, sir.
